title: Core Security Principles & the CIA Triad
summary: The foundational security concepts and governing principles that underpin all cybersecurity decisions in US government and diplomatic IT environments.
content:
The CIA triad is the cornerstone of information security. Confidentiality ensures only authorized parties can read data (achieved via encryption, access controls). Integrity ensures data is not altered without authorization (achieved via hashing, digital signatures, checksums). Availability ensures systems and data are accessible when needed (achieved via redundancy, backups, DDoS protection). A related extension adds Authentication and Non-repudiation (sometimes called the AAA or the "Parkerian Hexad" extensions).

• Least privilege: users, processes, and systems get only the minimum access required to perform their function and nothing more. This limits the blast radius of a compromised account.
• Defense in depth (layered security): no single control is trusted; overlapping layers (perimeter firewall, network segmentation, endpoint protection, encryption, user training) ensure that if one fails, others still protect the asset.
• Separation of duties: critical tasks are split so no single person can complete a sensitive action alone (e.g., one person requests a wire transfer, another approves it), reducing fraud and error.
• Need-to-know: even with proper clearance, access to specific information is granted only if required for one's assigned duties. Clearance level alone is insufficient.

Data classification in US government uses levels such as Unclassified, Confidential, Secret, and Top Secret, with additional caveats (e.g., SCI, NOFORN). Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) is the category for sensitive-but-unclassified data (replacing legacy markings like FOUO/SBU) and is governed by 32 CFR Part 2002 and NIST SP 800-171. Embassies and diplomatic posts handle CUI extensively (visa records, cables, personnel data) and must protect it per State Department and federal standards.

Zero Trust is the modern security model summarized as "never trust, always verify." It assumes no implicit trust based on network location (no "trusted internal network"). Every access request is continuously authenticated, authorized, and validated based on identity, device health, and context. The federal mandate is OMB M-22-09, aligned to the CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model and NIST SP 800-207.

These principles work together: least privilege and need-to-know enforce confidentiality; separation of duties supports integrity; defense in depth supports all three; and zero trust operationalizes least privilege at scale.
keyPoints: CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability; the three core goals of all security
Confidentiality via encryption/access control; Integrity via hashing/signatures; Availability via redundancy/backups
Least privilege grants minimum necessary access; limits damage from compromise
Defense in depth uses overlapping layers so one failure does not cause a breach
Separation of duties splits sensitive tasks so no one person acts alone
Need-to-know restricts access by duty even when clearance is sufficient
CUI is Controlled Unclassified Information governed by NIST SP 800-171 and 32 CFR 2002
US classification levels: Unclassified, Confidential, Secret, Top Secret (plus SCI/NOFORN caveats)
Zero Trust = 'never trust, always verify'; no trust based on network location
Federal Zero Trust mandate is OMB M-22-09, aligned to NIST SP 800-207 and CISA maturity model

flashcards.front: What does the CIA triad stand for?
flashcards.back: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability — the three core objectives of information security.

flashcards.front: Which CIA component does hashing primarily protect?
flashcards.back: Integrity — hashing detects unauthorized alteration of data.

flashcards.front: Define the principle of least privilege.
flashcards.back: Granting users, processes, and systems only the minimum access necessary to perform their function, limiting the blast radius of a compromise.

flashcards.front: What is defense in depth?
flashcards.back: A layered security strategy using multiple overlapping controls so that if one layer fails, others still protect the asset.

flashcards.front: What is separation of duties?
flashcards.back: Splitting a critical task among multiple people so no single individual can complete a sensitive action alone, reducing fraud and error.

flashcards.front: How does need-to-know differ from security clearance?
flashcards.back: Clearance grants eligibility to access a classification level; need-to-know further restricts access to only the specific information required for one's duties.

flashcards.front: What is CUI and what governs it?
flashcards.back: Controlled Unclassified Information — sensitive but unclassified data governed by 32 CFR Part 2002 and protected per NIST SP 800-171. It replaced markings like FOUO/SBU.

flashcards.front: List US classification levels from lowest to highest.
flashcards.back: Unclassified, Confidential, Secret, Top Secret (with caveats such as SCI and NOFORN).

flashcards.front: Summarize the Zero Trust model in one phrase.
flashcards.back: "Never trust, always verify" — no implicit trust based on network location; every request is continuously authenticated and authorized.

flashcards.front: Which federal documents mandate and define Zero Trust?
flashcards.back: OMB M-22-09 mandates it; NIST SP 800-207 defines the architecture; CISA provides the Zero Trust Maturity Model.

flashcards.front: Which CIA principle is targeted by a ransomware attack that encrypts files?
flashcards.back: Availability (and confidentiality if data is also exfiltrated) — the data becomes inaccessible to authorized users.

flashcards.front: Give an embassy example of separation of duties.
flashcards.back: One officer initiates a wire transfer or visa approval while a different officer reviews and authorizes it, so no single person controls the full transaction.

title: Cryptography, PKI & Secure Communications
summary: How encryption, hashing, certificates, and secure protocols protect diplomatic data in transit and at rest.
content:
Cryptography provides confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation. Symmetric encryption uses one shared secret key for both encryption and decryption (e.g., AES, the federal standard). It is fast and ideal for bulk data, but key distribution is the challenge. Asymmetric (public-key) encryption uses a mathematically linked key pair — a public key and a private key (e.g., RSA, ECC). Anything encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the private key, solving key distribution but being much slower. In practice, systems use hybrid encryption: asymmetric crypto to exchange a symmetric session key, then symmetric crypto for the actual data.

• Hashing vs encryption: encryption is reversible (you can decrypt with the key); hashing is a one-way function producing a fixed-length digest that cannot be reversed. Hashes (SHA-256, SHA-3) verify integrity — any change to input produces a completely different output. Hashing is used for password storage, file integrity, and digital signatures. Encryption protects confidentiality; hashing protects integrity.

Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) manages digital certificates and keys. A Certificate Authority (CA) is a trusted entity that issues and signs digital certificates binding a public key to an identity. A certificate (X.509) contains the subject's identity, public key, validity dates, and the CA's signature. The chain of trust runs from a trusted root CA, through intermediate CAs, down to the end-entity (leaf) certificate; your system trusts the leaf because it trusts the root that ultimately signed it. A self-signed certificate is signed by its own private key rather than a CA — useful for internal testing but untrusted by default because there is no external validation.

The TLS handshake establishes a secure session: the client and server agree on a cipher suite, the server presents its certificate (which the client validates against trusted CAs), and they negotiate keys. With modern TLS (1.3) using ephemeral Diffie-Hellman, both sides derive a shared session key without transmitting it. Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) means each session uses a unique ephemeral key, so compromise of the server's long-term private key cannot decrypt previously captured traffic.

Digital signatures provide integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation: the sender hashes the message and encrypts the hash with their private key; the recipient decrypts it with the sender's public key and compares hashes. Because only the sender holds the private key, they cannot later deny signing (non-repudiation). Key rotation periodically replaces cryptographic keys to limit exposure if a key is compromised. Tokenization replaces sensitive data (e.g., a PII field or PAN) with a non-sensitive surrogate token that has no exploitable value, with the real data stored in a secure vault — distinct from encryption because the token is not mathematically derived from the data.
keyPoints: Symmetric encryption (AES) uses one shared key — fast, but key distribution is the challenge
Asymmetric encryption (RSA, ECC) uses a public/private key pair — solves key distribution but is slower
Hybrid systems use asymmetric crypto to exchange a symmetric session key
Hashing is one-way and irreversible (SHA-256); encryption is reversible with a key
A Certificate Authority (CA) issues and signs X.509 certificates binding a public key to an identity
Chain of trust: root CA → intermediate CA → leaf certificate; trust flows from the root
Self-signed certificates are untrusted by default (no external CA validation)
Perfect Forward Secrecy uses ephemeral keys so a stolen private key cannot decrypt past sessions
Digital signatures provide integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation via private-key signing
Tokenization swaps sensitive data for a meaningless surrogate token stored in a secure vault

flashcards.front: Symmetric vs asymmetric encryption: key difference?
flashcards.back: Symmetric uses one shared secret key for encrypt/decrypt (fast); asymmetric uses a public/private key pair (slower, solves key distribution).

flashcards.front: What is the US/federal symmetric encryption standard?
flashcards.back: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard), e.g., AES-256.

flashcards.front: How does hashing differ from encryption?
flashcards.back: Hashing is a one-way, irreversible function producing a fixed-length digest (integrity); encryption is reversible with a key (confidentiality).

flashcards.front: What does a Certificate Authority (CA) do?
flashcards.back: It is a trusted entity that issues and digitally signs X.509 certificates, binding a public key to a verified identity.

flashcards.front: Describe the chain of trust in PKI.
flashcards.back: Trust flows from a trusted root CA, through intermediate CAs, down to the end-entity (leaf) certificate; the leaf is trusted because the root ultimately signed it.

flashcards.front: Why is a self-signed certificate untrusted by default?
flashcards.back: It is signed by its own private key with no external CA validation, so there is no third party vouching for the identity.

flashcards.front: What is Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)?
flashcards.back: Each session uses a unique ephemeral key, so compromise of the server's long-term private key cannot decrypt previously captured traffic.

flashcards.front: What three security properties does a digital signature provide?
flashcards.back: Integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation.

flashcards.front: How is a digital signature created and verified?
flashcards.back: Sender hashes the message and encrypts the hash with their private key; recipient decrypts with the sender's public key and compares hashes.

flashcards.front: What happens during a TLS handshake?
flashcards.back: Client and server agree on a cipher suite, the server presents its certificate (validated against trusted CAs), and they negotiate a shared session key (ephemeral DH in TLS 1.3).

flashcards.front: Why use a hybrid (asymmetric + symmetric) approach in TLS?
flashcards.back: Asymmetric crypto securely exchanges a symmetric session key; symmetric crypto then encrypts bulk data quickly.

flashcards.front: What is tokenization and how does it differ from encryption?
flashcards.back: Tokenization replaces sensitive data with a non-sensitive surrogate token stored in a vault; unlike encryption, the token is not mathematically derived from the original data.

flashcards.front: Why perform key rotation?
flashcards.back: To periodically replace cryptographic keys, limiting the amount of data exposed if a key is compromised.

title: Authentication, Access Control & Network Defense
summary: How identities are verified and access is controlled, plus the network defenses that protect embassy and government infrastructure.
content:
Authentication verifies identity using factors in three categories: something you know (password, PIN), something you have (smart card, token, phone), and something you are (biometrics — fingerprint, iris, face). Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) requires two or more factors from different categories; two passwords are not MFA. Government environments commonly use PIV/CAC smart cards (something you have) combined with a PIN (something you know). Additional factor types include somewhere you are (geolocation) and something you do (behavioral).

• FIDO2/passkeys: a phishing-resistant, passwordless standard using public-key cryptography. The private key never leaves the user's device/authenticator, and authentication is bound to the legitimate site's origin, defeating phishing and credential replay. CISA strongly recommends phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/PIV) for high-value accounts.
• RADIUS vs TACACS+: both are AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) protocols. RADIUS combines authentication and authorization, encrypts only the password, and uses UDP — common for network access (Wi-Fi, VPN). TACACS+ (Cisco) separates AAA functions, encrypts the entire payload, and uses TCP — favored for device administration.
• RBAC vs ABAC: Role-Based Access Control grants permissions based on a user's role (e.g., "Consular Officer"). Attribute-Based Access Control grants access based on evaluated attributes/policies (user department, device security posture, time of day, data classification), enabling fine-grained, context-aware decisions central to zero trust.

Password security: account lockout disables an account after a set number of failed attempts to thwart brute force. Strong password policy emphasizes length and complexity; salting adds a unique random value to each password before hashing so identical passwords produce different hashes, defeating precomputed rainbow tables.

Network security layers defenses. Firewalls filter traffic by rules; an allowlist (default-deny) permits only explicitly approved traffic and is far stronger than a blocklist. An IDS detects and alerts on suspicious activity (passive); an IPS detects and actively blocks it (inline). Network Access Control (NAC) enforces device compliance (patch level, AV) before granting network access. A VPN creates an encrypted tunnel over untrusted networks; IPsec is the standard suite for secure site-to-site and remote-access VPNs.

Architectural and monitoring controls: an air gap physically isolates classified/critical systems from unsecured networks. A DMZ is a buffer subnet hosting public-facing services, segmented from the internal network. Sandboxing runs untrusted code in an isolated environment. A honeypot is a decoy system to lure and study attackers. EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) monitors endpoints; XDR extends detection across endpoints, network, cloud, and email. A SIEM aggregates and correlates logs for centralized monitoring and alerting. DLP (Data Loss Prevention) detects and blocks unauthorized exfiltration of sensitive data such as CUI.
keyPoints: MFA factors: something you know, something you have, something you are; MFA needs 2+ different categories
Government uses PIV/CAC smart card (have) + PIN (know) as standard MFA
FIDO2/passkeys are phishing-resistant, passwordless, using public-key crypto bound to site origin
RADIUS (UDP, encrypts only password, combines auth/authz) vs TACACS+ (TCP, encrypts all, separates AAA)
RBAC grants access by role; ABAC grants access by evaluated attributes/policies (context-aware)
Salting adds unique random data before hashing to defeat rainbow tables; account lockout stops brute force
Allowlist (default-deny) is stronger than blocklist; IDS detects/alerts, IPS detects and blocks inline
NAC checks device compliance before network access; VPN/IPsec encrypts traffic over untrusted networks
Air gap physically isolates systems; DMZ buffers public services; sandbox isolates untrusted code; honeypot is a decoy
EDR monitors endpoints, XDR correlates across layers, SIEM aggregates logs, DLP blocks data exfiltration

flashcards.front: Name the three authentication factor categories.
flashcards.back: Something you know (password/PIN), something you have (token/smart card), something you are (biometric).

flashcards.front: Is using two passwords considered MFA?
flashcards.back: No. MFA requires two or more factors from different categories; two passwords are both 'something you know.'

flashcards.front: What standard government credential provides 'something you have'?
flashcards.back: A PIV or CAC smart card, typically combined with a PIN for MFA.

flashcards.front: Why are FIDO2/passkeys phishing-resistant?
flashcards.back: They use public-key crypto bound to the legitimate site's origin; the private key never leaves the device, so credentials can't be phished or replayed on a fake site.

flashcards.front: RADIUS vs TACACS+: name two differences.
flashcards.back: RADIUS uses UDP, encrypts only the password, combines auth/authz; TACACS+ uses TCP, encrypts the full payload, separates AAA functions.

flashcards.front: RBAC vs ABAC?
flashcards.back: RBAC grants access based on a user's role; ABAC grants access based on evaluated attributes/policies (department, device posture, time, classification).

flashcards.front: What is salting and what attack does it defeat?
flashcards.back: Adding a unique random value to each password before hashing so identical passwords hash differently; it defeats precomputed rainbow tables.

flashcards.front: IDS vs IPS?
flashcards.back: An IDS passively detects and alerts on suspicious activity; an IPS sits inline and actively blocks it.

flashcards.front: Why is an allowlist stronger than a blocklist?
flashcards.back: An allowlist is default-deny — only explicitly approved traffic is permitted; a blocklist allows everything not explicitly banned, missing unknown threats.

flashcards.front: What does NAC enforce?
flashcards.back: Network Access Control verifies a device meets security policy (patches, AV, configuration) before granting network access.

flashcards.front: What is an air gap?
flashcards.back: Physical isolation of a system from unsecured networks, used for classified or critical systems.

flashcards.front: What is a DMZ?
flashcards.back: A buffer subnet hosting public-facing services, segmented from the internal network to limit exposure.

flashcards.front: Difference between EDR and XDR?
flashcards.back: EDR detects and responds to threats on endpoints; XDR extends and correlates detection across endpoints, network, cloud, and email.

flashcards.front: What does a SIEM do?
flashcards.back: Aggregates and correlates logs from across the environment for centralized monitoring, alerting, and investigation.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of DLP?
flashcards.back: Data Loss Prevention detects and blocks unauthorized transfer or exfiltration of sensitive data such as CUI.

flashcards.front: What is a honeypot?
flashcards.back: A decoy system designed to lure attackers so their behavior can be detected and studied without risk to real assets.

title: Threats, Vulnerability Management & Security Operations
summary: Common attacks against diplomatic systems and the processes used to find weaknesses, respond to incidents, and operate securely.
content:
Social engineering manipulates people rather than technology. Phishing is fraudulent email seeking credentials or malware execution; vishing uses voice/phone calls; smishing uses SMS text; whaling targets senior executives or officials (e.g., an ambassador). Other human-vector attacks include tailgating (following an authorized person through a secure door) and pretexting (inventing a scenario to gain trust). Diplomatic personnel are high-value targets for foreign intelligence, making awareness training critical.

• Malware and code attacks: ransomware encrypts data and demands payment; other malware includes viruses, worms, trojans, and spyware. SQL injection (SQLi) inserts malicious SQL into input fields to manipulate a database. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by others. CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tricks an authenticated user's browser into making unwanted requests. A buffer overflow writes data beyond allocated memory to crash a system or execute code. Privilege escalation gains higher access than authorized (vertical or horizontal).

• Network and credential attacks: Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) intercepts communications; SSL stripping downgrades HTTPS to HTTP. ARP spoofing and DNS spoofing poison address resolution to redirect traffic. Brute-force tries all combinations; dictionary attacks use wordlists; credential stuffing reuses breached username/password pairs; rainbow tables use precomputed hashes (defeated by salting).

• Advanced and human threats: a supply-chain attack compromises a trusted vendor or software update (e.g., SolarWinds) to reach many victims. An insider threat is a malicious or negligent authorized user. A zero-day exploits an unknown, unpatched vulnerability with no available fix.

Vulnerability management: a CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) is a unique identifier for a specific known flaw; CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) scores its severity 0–10. A vulnerability scan is automated and identifies known weaknesses; a penetration test is a hands-on simulated attack that exploits them to prove impact. Red teams attack, blue teams defend, and purple teaming combines them. Hardening reduces attack surface by disabling unnecessary services, applying patches, and enforcing secure configurations (per CIS Benchmarks / DISA STIGs). The NIST Cybersecurity Framework organizes activity into functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover (with Govern added in CSF 2.0).

Security operations: the incident response lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61) is Preparation, Detection & Analysis, Containment, Eradication & Recovery, and Post-Incident (lessons learned). The 3-2-1 backup rule keeps 3 copies of data on 2 different media with 1 offsite. MDM (Mobile Device Management) enforces policy on mobile devices, important for BYOD. Audit logging records who did what and when for accountability and forensics. Secure media disposal uses secure wipe (overwriting), degaussing (magnetic erasure), or physical destruction. Certificate revocation is checked via CRL (Certificate Revocation List) or OCSP (real-time status). Two-person integrity (TPI) requires two authorized people for the most sensitive actions, and COMSEC (Communications Security) governs the protection of cryptographic material and secure communications — central to embassy operations.
keyPoints: Phishing (email), vishing (voice), smishing (SMS), whaling (targets executives/officials)
SQLi targets databases; XSS injects scripts into web pages; CSRF abuses an authenticated session
MITM intercepts traffic; SSL stripping downgrades HTTPS; ARP/DNS spoofing redirect via poisoned resolution
Brute-force/dictionary/credential-stuffing/rainbow tables attack passwords; salting defeats rainbow tables
Supply-chain attacks compromise trusted vendors; zero-days exploit unknown unpatched flaws; insiders abuse legitimate access
CVE identifies a specific flaw; CVSS scores its severity 0–10
Vulnerability scan finds known weaknesses (automated); pen test exploits them to prove impact (hands-on)
NIST CSF functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover (Govern added in 2.0)
Incident response lifecycle: Preparation, Detection/Analysis, Containment, Eradication/Recovery, Post-Incident
3-2-1 backups, secure wipe/degauss for disposal, CRL/OCSP for revocation, two-person integrity and COMSEC for sensitive material

flashcards.front: Distinguish phishing, vishing, smishing, and whaling.
flashcards.back: Phishing = fraudulent email; vishing = voice/phone; smishing = SMS text; whaling = phishing aimed at senior executives or officials.

flashcards.front: What is tailgating?
flashcards.back: A physical social-engineering attack where someone follows an authorized person through a secure door without their own credentials.

flashcards.front: What does SQL injection attack?
flashcards.back: A database — it inserts malicious SQL into input fields to read, modify, or destroy data or bypass authentication.

flashcards.front: XSS vs CSRF?
flashcards.back: XSS injects malicious scripts into pages other users view; CSRF tricks an authenticated user's browser into making unwanted requests on their behalf.

flashcards.front: What is a buffer overflow?
flashcards.back: Writing data beyond an allocated memory buffer to crash a program or execute arbitrary code.

flashcards.front: What is SSL stripping?
flashcards.back: A MITM technique that downgrades a victim's HTTPS connection to unencrypted HTTP to intercept data.

flashcards.front: Credential stuffing vs brute force?
flashcards.back: Credential stuffing reuses username/password pairs from prior breaches; brute force systematically tries many combinations.

flashcards.front: What is a zero-day?
flashcards.back: An attack exploiting a previously unknown vulnerability for which no patch yet exists.

flashcards.front: What is a supply-chain attack? Give an example.
flashcards.back: Compromising a trusted vendor or software update to reach its customers (e.g., the SolarWinds compromise).

flashcards.front: CVE vs CVSS?
flashcards.back: CVE is a unique identifier for a specific known vulnerability; CVSS is the 0–10 scoring system rating its severity.

flashcards.front: Vulnerability scan vs penetration test?
flashcards.back: A scan is automated detection of known weaknesses; a pen test is a hands-on simulated attack that exploits them to demonstrate real impact.

flashcards.front: What do red, blue, and purple teams do?
flashcards.back: Red team attacks (offense), blue team defends, purple team combines both to improve defenses collaboratively.

flashcards.front: List the five (six) NIST CSF functions.
flashcards.back: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover — plus Govern in CSF 2.0.

flashcards.front: What are the phases of the NIST incident response lifecycle?
flashcards.back: Preparation; Detection & Analysis; Containment, Eradication & Recovery; Post-Incident Activity (lessons learned).

flashcards.front: State the 3-2-1 backup rule.
flashcards.back: Keep 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy stored offsite.

flashcards.front: CRL vs OCSP?
flashcards.back: Both check certificate revocation: a CRL is a downloaded list of revoked certificates; OCSP queries revocation status in real time.

flashcards.front: How is sensitive media securely disposed of?
flashcards.back: Secure wipe (overwriting), degaussing (magnetic erasure for magnetic media), or physical destruction.

flashcards.front: What is two-person integrity (TPI)?
flashcards.back: A control requiring two authorized individuals to be present and act together for the most sensitive operations (e.g., handling COMSEC keying material).

flashcards.front: What is COMSEC?
flashcards.back: Communications Security — the protection of cryptographic material, keys, and secure communications, critical to embassy and diplomatic operations.
